title: Dynamic scaling and Family-Vicsek universality in the Hubbard model at infinite temperature
authors: Cătălin Paşcu Moca, Doru Sticlet, Balázs Dóra
abstract: We study Family-Vicsek scaling of charge, spin, and energy fluctuations in the one-dimensional Hubbard model at infinite temperature. Using a quantum generating function approach, we compute time-dependent cumulants of transferred conserved quantities and analyze how the corresponding roughness depends on subsystem size and time. We start by focusing on a single interface at half the chain and determine the transport exponents. Then we turn to fluctuations of a small finite interval and study the Family-Vicsek universality of fluctuations over an extended timescale. We find that the long-time scaling behavior is controlled by integrability. In the free limit, charge, spin, and energy all display ballistic transport. In the interacting integrable Hubbard chain, charge and spin cross over to a KPZ scaling regime, while the energy sector remains ballistic. Once integrability is broken by a next-nearest-neighbor density interaction, the long-time dynamics becomes diffusive in all sectors. In every case we also observe a short-time microscopic regime with apparently universal ballistic growth before the hydrodynamic scaling window sets in. The Family-Vicsek setup allows us to determine the growth, the saturation as well as the dynamical exponents.
summary: This theoretical study uses Quantum Generating Functions and Family-Vicsek scaling analysis to characterize how charge, spin, and energy fluctuations spread in the 1D Hubbard model. The key finding is that integrability dictates the long-time transport regime: integrable models exhibit anomalous (KPZ) or ballistic spreading, while breaking this symmetry drives the system towards classical diffusive behavior.
main_problem: The study investigates Family-Vicsek (FV) scaling of charge, spin, and energy fluctuations in the one-dimensional Hubbard model at infinite temperature.
main_result: The long-time dynamics are critically controlled by integrability: integrable cases show KPZ or ballistic transport for certain sectors, while breaking integrability leads to diffusive behavior across all measured quantities.
method: The analysis employs a Quantum Generating Function (QGF) approach combined with Family-Vicsek scaling analysis to compute time-dependent cumulants of conserved quantities without reconstructing the full probability distribution.
model_or_system: The system is the one-dimensional Fermi-Hubbard model, characterized by hopping ($J$) and onsite interaction ($U$). The Hamiltonian can be perturbed by adding a next-nearest-neighbor density interaction ($	ext{H}_{	ext{NNN}}$) to break integrability.
key_observables: Charge ($	ext{N}$), spin ($	ext{S}_z$), and energy ($	ext{E}$) fluctuations, quantified by their second cumulants ($\kappa^2$) and analyzed via the roughness function $W(\ell, t)$.
important_parameters: The key parameters are the interaction strength ($U$), the perturbation strength ($V$, related to $	ext{H}_{	ext{NNN}}$), and the system size/time scales.
main_assumptions: The use of the QGF approach allows for the direct extraction of cumulants, bypassing the need to reconstruct the full quantum distribution function.
figures_summary:
Figures illustrate Family-Vicsek scaling collapses for charge, spin, and energy roughness under different parameter regimes (e.g., $U=0$ vs $U
eq 0$, or $V=0$ vs $V 
eq 0$), confirming predicted power-law growth exponents.
paper_structure:
The paper systematically analyzes the scaling behavior for charge, spin, and energy separately, contrasting the results obtained in the free limit ($U=0$), the interacting integrable case ($V=0$), and the non-integrable regime ($V 
eq 0$).
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful diagnostic tool (FV scaling) to map out the transition between different universality classes of transport (ballistic, KPZ, diffusive) in strongly correlated quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Half-chain second moment κ(Q) 2 (t) for charge (top) and spin (bottom) at U = 0 and V = 2 (blue), U = 0 and V = 2 (orange), U = 2 and V = 0 (green), and U = 2 and V = 2 (gray). The dashed lines show the expected power-law growth with exponents 1/z = 1 (ballistic), 2/3 (KPZ), and 1/2 (diffusive). For U = 0 and V = 0 the exact analytical results from Eqs. (27) and (28) are shown (dashed) and match the numerics. The numerical data are obtained for L = 200 sites and a small counting field r = 0.05 in Eq. (16). The crossover time t(c) ∼1/(2J) is indicated by the vertical red arrow.

FIG. 2. Half-chain second moment κ(E) 2 (t) as function of time. The dashed lines show the expected power-law growth with exponents 1/z = 1 (ballistic) and 1/2 (diffusive). The numerical data are obtained for L = 200 sites and a small counting field λ = 0.05. The crossover time t(c) ∼1/(J) is indicated by the vertical red arrow.

FIG. 3. Family–Vicsek scaling collapse for the charge rough- ness WN(ℓ, t) for U = 0 and V = 0. The data are obtained for L = 400 sites and a small counting field r = 0.05 in Eq. (16). The dashed lines show the expected asymptotic power-law growth with β = 1/2 and saturation with α = 1/2. The crossover time scale is consistent with the analytical estimate from Eq. (55). The inset shows the same data unrescaled, illustrating the three regimes separated by the crossover times t(c) and t∗(ℓ).

FIG. 4. Family–Vicsek scaling collapse for the charge rough- ness WN(ℓ, t) for U = 2.0 and V = 0, (interacting integrable model) indicating the KPZ universality class, with dynamical exponent z = 3/2, growth exponent β = 1/3, and roughness exponent α = 1/2. The black dashed lines show the expected asymptotic power-law growth with β = 1/3. The inset shows the same data unrescaled.
